text: The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, with three ports placed in the midline. The surgeon reduces the hernia contents and incises the peritoneum lateral to the right posterior rectus sheath. Dissection is performed above the transversalis fascia to create a large preperitoneal pocket extending from the inguinal region to the subdiaphragmatic space. The hernia sac is carefully dissected away from surrounding tissues, with dissection proceeding from medial to lateral towards the paravertebral muscles. Fibrosis is encountered, and some unavoidable peritoneal tears occur. Hemostasis is achieved before closing the defect.

The surgeon begins primary closure of the transverse defect, measuring approximately 16 cm wide by 18 cm long, using a zero V-lock suture. The closure starts at the posterior aspect between the tenth and eleventh ribs, where significant tension is noted. The first suture is tightened gradually to re-approximate the edges. A second V-lock suture is placed at the mid portion of the defect, where tension is greatest, and tightened gently after each bite. The third suture is started at the medial aspect, under the least tension, allowing easier closure. A fourth suture bridges the gap between the first two sutures, completing the closure with overlapping bites.

A self-gripping mesh measuring 16 by 12 centimeters is centered over the suture line to alleviate tension from the defect closure. A larger 24 by 20 centimeter mid-weight microporous polypropylene mesh is then placed over the defect. The mesh is secured with a trans-fascial suture at the medial aspect and unrolled medially to laterally, lying flat against the abdominal wall with corners tucked in. Additional trans-fascial sutures and a running V-lock suture are used to center the mesh, while fibrin glue and Tuo interrupted vicryl sutures ensure the mesh lies flat against the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon closes peritoneal tears using vicryl sutures and secures the peritoneal flap with a running 3-0 V-lock horizontal mattress stitch, ensuring the mesh remains completely extra-peritoneal. The surgical field shows no signs of hernia recurrence post-operatively.
